real_meaning: ～ところ expresses a specific point in time relative to an action — 'just about to,' 'in the middle of,' or 'just finished,' depending on the verb form attached. The core feeling is a snapshot: you're zooming in on one precise moment in an action's lifecycle. It's not about the action itself but about WHERE you are in that action's timeline.

formation.rule: Verb dictionary form + ところ (just about to do); Verb ている + ところ (in the middle of doing); Verb た + ところ (just finished doing). Examples: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるところ (just about to eat)、<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べているところ (in the middle of eating)、<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたところ (just finished eating).
formation.common_mistake: Using the た-form when you mean 'just about to' — e.g. writing ×<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたところだ to mean 'I'm about to eat' instead of ○<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるところだ. The た-form shifts the meaning entirely to 'I just ate.'

sentences.jp: ちょうど<ruby>家<rt>いえ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>出<rt>で</rt></ruby>るところだったのに、<ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>り<ruby>出<rt>だ</rt></ruby>した。
sentences.translation: I was just about to leave the house when it started raining.
sentences.notes: The past form だったのに adds a nuance of frustration — 'I was right at that moment and then this happened,' a very natural use of <ruby>辞<rt>じ</rt></ruby><ruby>書<rt>しょ</rt></ruby><ruby>形<rt>けい</rt></ruby>ところ in storytelling.

sentences.jp: すみません、<ruby>今<rt>いま</rt></ruby>ちょっと<ruby>手<rt>て</rt></ruby>が<ruby>離<rt>はな</rt></ruby>せないんです。レポートを<ruby>書<rt>か</rt></ruby>いているところで。
sentences.translation: Sorry, I can't step away right now. I'm in the middle of writing a report.
sentences.notes: ているところ gives a precise 'right this moment' feeling that just ている alone lacks — it emphasizes the interruption.

sentences.jp: <ruby>只今<rt>ただいま</rt></ruby>、<ruby>担当者<rt>たんとうしゃ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>確認<rt>かくにん</rt></ruby>しているところでございます。<ruby>少々<rt>しょうしょう</rt></ruby>お<ruby>待<rt>ま</rt></ruby>ちください。
sentences.translation: The person in charge is currently in the process of checking on that. Please wait a moment.
sentences.notes: ているところでございます is a standard business/service phrase used to report an ongoing action to a customer, signaling it is actively in progress right now.

sentences.jp: <ruby>怒<rt>おこ</rt></ruby>ろうとしていたところを、<ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>笑顔<rt>えがお</rt></ruby>で<ruby>謝<rt>あやま</rt></ruby>られてしまった。
sentences.translation: Just as I was about to get angry, she apologized with a smile and I couldn't help it.
sentences.notes: ところを (with を instead of だ) means 'at the very moment of,' often used when something interrupts or catches you at that instant — a subtler construction that surprises many learners.

confused_with.contrast: Both can describe being in the middle of something, but they feel different. ～ているところ is a frozen snapshot — it zooms in on a single precise moment and often implies an interruption or situation at that instant. ～<ruby>途中<rt>とちゅう</rt></ruby>で describes the midpoint of a process more broadly, without the snapshot feeling, and doesn't require any interruption. Minimal pair: <ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ているところに<ruby>電話<rt>でんわ</rt></ruby>がかかってきた (A call came in right at the moment I was watching a movie — emphasis on that instant) vs. <ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ている<ruby>途中<rt>とちゅう</rt></ruby>で<ruby>電話<rt>でんわ</rt></ruby>がかかってきた (A call came in partway through the movie — emphasis on mid-process). Use ところ when the precise moment matters; use <ruby>途中<rt>とちゅう</rt></ruby>で when the midpoint of an extended process is the focus.

confusion_set.use_when: You want to pinpoint WHERE in an action's timeline you are — just before, right during, or just after.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>今<rt>いま</rt></ruby>から<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるところです。(I'm just about to eat right now.)

confusion_set.use_when: You just finished something and want to say 'I just did X' — overlaps with たところ but emphasizes the freshness or recency more strongly and doesn't require the immediate-moment snapshot.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>日本<rt>にほん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>来<rt>き</rt></ruby>たばかりで、まだ<ruby>何<rt>なに</rt></ruby>もわからない。(I just arrived in Japan so I still don't know anything.)

confusion_set.use_when: Something happens right in the heat of an ongoing action — stronger than ているところ, implies the action was intense or important and was disrupted at its peak.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>会議<rt>かいぎ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>最中<rt>さいちゅう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>地震<rt>じしん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>起<rt>お</rt></ruby>きた。(An earthquake struck right in the middle of the meeting.)

bunpro_tip: Think of ところ literally as 'place' — you're identifying the 'place' on the timeline of an action: before it (dictionary form), inside it (ている), or right after it (た). When you see a BunPro fill-in, first ask yourself: where on the action's timeline is the speaker standing?
